The same gentle nature that leads some faithful people into friendship with those openly opposed to religion can be very dangerous for them. They will be drawn to overlook and approve of behavior and conversations they should strongly condemn. Wherever a good person goes, they should take their faith with them and not be ashamed to acknowledge it even when surrounded by those who do not value it. Jehoshaphat did not leave behind his love and respect for the word of the Lord in Jerusalem; he openly declared it and tried to bring it into Ahab's court. Ahab’s prophets, wanting to please Jehoshaphat, used the name of Jehovah; they told him to go up. But even false prophets cannot perfectly imitate true ones, so that someone with spiritual understanding can recognize their deception. One faithful prophet of the Lord was worth more than them all. People focused on worldly matters have always been equally unreasonable in their views of religion. They want preachers to tailor their teachings to the current trends and the preferences of the listeners, yet still insist on adding, 'Thus says the Lord,' to words that people want them to say. They are quick to criticize someone as rude and foolish for refusing to compromise to protect their own interests or deceive others.

The greatest kindness we can show someone heading down a dangerous path is to warn them of the danger. To leave unrepentant criminals without excuse, and to provide a helpful lesson for others, Micaiah shared his vision. This account is presented in human terms: we should not imagine that God is ever put under new counsel or that he needs to consult with angels or any creation about how he should proceed, or that he is the author of sin or the cause of anyone telling or believing a lie. Micaiah did not retaliate against Zedekiah’s blow, but since he boasted about having the Spirit—as those commonly do who know least about the Holy Spirit's workings—the true prophet left him to be convinced of his error by what happened. Those who refuse to have their mistakes corrected in time by the word of God will be shown the truth when it is too late, through God’s judgments. We should feel ashamed of what we call trials if we consider what God's servants have endured. However, it may not be better for us to avoid trouble; we are more easily tempted and bribed into unfaithfulness and conformity to the world than driven toward them.

Ahab intended to betray Jehoshaphat to danger so that he could protect himself. See what happens to those who join with wicked people. How can it be expected that someone will be loyal to a friend when they have been disloyal to God? He had said in flattery to Ahab, 'I am as you are,' and now he was indeed considered one of them. Those who associate with wrongdoers risk sharing in their punishments. Through Jehoshaphat's deliverance, God let him know that even though he was displeased with him, he had not abandoned him. God is a friend who will not fail us when other friends do. No person should think they can hide from God’s judgment. God directed the arrow to strike Ahab; those whom God has condemned to death cannot escape. Ahab lived long enough to see part of Micaiah's prophecy fulfilled. He had time to feel himself dying; with what horror must he have thought about the wickedness he had committed!
Jehoshaphat’s reign appeared to be one of the best, both in terms of piety and prosperity. He pleased God, and God blessed him.

Ahaziah's reign was very short, less than two years; some sinners are quickly dealt with by God. He is described as having a very bad character; he did not listen to instruction, took no warning, but followed the example of his wicked father and the counsel of his even more wicked mother, Jezebel, who was still alive. Miserable are the children who not only inherit a sinful nature from their parents but are taught by them to increase it; and equally unhappy are those parents who help to condemn their children’s souls. Hardened sinners rush forward, unafraid and unmoved, on paths that have led others into everlasting misery.
